summary: Jeff Atwood shares Philip Chu's 'Seven Habits of Highly Effective Programmers' through a series of illuminating anecdotes from Chu's career. The examples demonstrate common pitfalls in software development: over-planning that delays coding, reluctance to release early for testing, and writing code so unmemorable that you don't recognize it later. Atwood endorses Chu's writing as witty and eloquent, recommending his other articles as well. The post serves as a brief curation of wisdom from a fellow programmer's hard-won experiences.
key_points: Extended design meetings often prove wasteful when code quickly diverges from original plans
Early QA involvement forces developers to address real user-blocking issues rather than what seems technically important
Developers naturally gravitate toward 'hard' problems while neglecting mundane but critical functionality like UI and hardware compatibility
Code you wrote yourself can become unrecognizable without adequate documentation
Getting external feedback early prevents developers from going down unproductive paths
money_quotes: Within two weeks, my part of the project had no resemblance to the original design, and the first wireless connection test two months later revealed a fundamental misunderstanding of the wireless protocol.

Left to my own devices, I would have worked on what seemed to be the harder and more important core 3D issues, and probably delayed too long on seemingly mundane issues like the user interface, load-save functionality, and compatabilaty with all the varieties of consumer hardware we were planning to support.

At first, I thought it was a mess and didn't know what was going on in there. Then I gradually figured out what the code was doing, and I grudgingly admitted the code wasn't too bad. And then I eventually realized that I had written the code two years ago.
Note to self: need more comments.
The resulting bombardment of bug reports forced me to immediately focus on the problems that prevented the testers from exercising the application in any meaningful way.
key_insight: Real-world programming wisdom comes from painful experience: ship early, get feedback often, and document your code because even you won't remember what it does.
